steps.title: Step 1 — find the normal reaction N
steps.body: Resolve forces **perpendicular to the contact surface** first. On a horizontal surface with no other vertical forces, $N = mg$. On a slope inclined at $\theta$ to the horizontal, $N = mg\cos\theta$.

steps.title: Step 2 — find the maximum available friction
steps.body: Compute $F_{\max} = \mu N$ using the coefficient of friction $\mu$ for the two surfaces in contact. This is the *most* friction can ever supply — not necessarily what it is actually supplying.

steps.title: Step 3 — decide whether the object moves
steps.body: Compare the force trying to cause motion (along the surface) with $F_{\max}$. If it is **less than or equal to** $F_{\max}$, friction can balance it and the object stays in equilibrium. If it **exceeds** $F_{\max}$, the object accelerates.

steps.title: Step 4 — set F correctly in your equations
steps.body: If the object is in equilibrium (not on the point of moving), find the actual friction $F$ from the equilibrium equations — it will be less than $\mu N$. If the object is sliding, or explicitly on the point of slipping, use $F = \mu N$ exactly in your $F = ma$ equation.
